steps.n: Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning more intense
The ت at the end shows this is about a feminine subject. In this ayah, it's describing what happened to يَدَآ (the hands)
The root letters for 'hand' were connected with fatha marks to make yada (hand), which is the object receiving the action of perishing
Added alif at the end to show this means 'two hands' instead of just one hand
The alif at the end was lengthened to show this word is the subject being acted upon (like saying 'hands were...')
Connected the root letters and added basic vowels to make it pronounceable

steps.m: my father (father of أَبِى لَهَبٍ - Abu Lahab)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show possession (meaning 'my'). Changed the ُ to ِ before ي. This word is part of the name Abu Lahab, literally meaning 'father of flame'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from 'blazed' (action) to 'flame' (thing) by making it a noun

steps.m: flame/fire (used as part of Abu Lahab's name)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra at the end. In this ayah, it's part of the name Abu Lahab (Father of Flame), referring to a person who opposed the Prophet ﷺ.
Added basic past tense vowels and connected the letters
Adding shadda (doubling) on ب makes the meaning stronger and more intense
Added وَ (and) to connect this to the previous statement
This is the basic negative word that makes something 'not' happen. In this ayah, it's used with أَغْنَىٰ (availed/benefited) to say something 'did not benefit.'
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this is a past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning to show making someone else rich. The ي changed to ى because it's at the end.
Added the small alif (ٰ) to show this is the final form. In this ayah, this word means that the person's wealth didn't benefit or help them.
This is a preposition showing separation or distance
Adding هُ means 'him'. In this ayah, it refers back to Abu Lahab, showing what didn't benefit him.
Root letters م-و-ل got short vowels (َ ْ) to form the noun meaning 'wealth'
Added damma to show this is the subject doing the action
Added هُۥ to show possession (his). This word is the subject of أَغْنَىٰ (to avail/benefit), showing what did not benefit him.
Added fatha to show the correct pronunciation
Added ما which is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it connects to the verb كَسَبَ (he earned) to mean 'what he earned'

steps.m: to gain, earn, or acquire through effort
he earned/acquired (what he earned is referenced in مَآ أَغْنَىٰ)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fathas to show this happened in the past. This verb is connected to مَآ (what) in the ayah, showing what he earned didn't benefit him.

steps.m: to burn or be exposed to heat

steps.n: The root letters were connected with vowels (َ) to make a past tense verb meaning 'he burned'. The ي changes to ى at the end.
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense, with sukun on middle letter
Added سَ at beginning to show this will happen in the future
Added ى with alif on top at end to show proper spelling of verb
The weak letter و changes to ا, and the meaning focuses specifically on fire rather than light

steps.m: a fire (object of سَيَصْلَىٰ - he will enter)
steps.n: Adding tanwin alif makes it indefinite and shows it's being acted upon. This word is what is being entered into by the verb سَيَصْلَىٰ (he will enter/burn in).
This is a particle used to show possession or attribution. It's in the accusative case here.
Added fatha at the end because it's describing نَارًا (fire) which is in the accusative case. This word tells us what kind of fire - a fire possessing flames.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic noun. This word describes what type of fire (نَارًا) it is - one having (ذَاتَ) flames.
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is an indefinite noun (like saying 'a flame' instead of 'the flame')
Adding ة makes the word feminine, changing meaning to woman
Added damma to show this is the subject doing the action
Added possessive pronoun هُ meaning 'his'
Added conjunction وَ meaning 'and' and changed initial alif to waṣla form. This word is the subject of حَمَّالَةَ (carrier) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fathas to make a basic past-tense verb
Added a shadda and alif to show someone who does this action intensively
Added ة to show this is a woman doing the action

steps.m: female who carries a lot (object of وَٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this describes the woman mentioned before (his wife)

steps.m: firewood (the material being carried by حَمَّالَةَ)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the noun for the actual material. This word is what's being carried by حَمَّالَةَ (carrier) in this ayah.
The kasra ending shows this describes what kind of carrier (حَمَّالَةَ) she is - a carrier of firewood
Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - referring to the particular firewood she carries, not just any firewood
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it as 'fi'
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation. This word connects to جِيدِهَا (her neck) to show where the rope is.
Connected the root letters and added the basic vowel pattern to form the noun
Added -i sound at the end because it's connected to 'rope' (showing ownership) and comes after 'in'
Added هَا to show the neck belongs to her (Abu Lahab's wife)
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun form meaning 'rope'

steps.m: a rope (that is around عُنُقِهَا her neck)
steps.n: Adding tanween damma (ٌ) makes this an indefinite noun meaning 'a rope'. This word is part of a longer description of a rope that is around someone's neck.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of
The shadda (ّ) is added to connect this word smoothly with the next word مَّسَدٍۭ (palm fiber). This connection emphasizes that the rope is specifically made from palm fiber.
The root refers to the action of twisting something tightly
Connected the root letters with fatha to create a noun for the thing that results from twisting/braiding
Added genitive case ending to show 'of' relationship
The word has a shadda (doubled letter) added to make it stronger, showing the rope is very tightly twisted. It has tanween (double dots under) because it comes after the word 'of' (مِن)
